g.data-narration: Welcome to Storage Management! This topic covers how operating systems manage memory from fast CPU registers to slow magnetic tapes.
Storage management is critical because the CPU can only work with data in main memory, but RAM is limited and volatile.
g.text: • CPU can only execute instructions in main memory
• RAM is limited in size - not all processes can fit at once
• Multiple processes must share memory efficiently

g.data-narration: We'll study the storage hierarchy, memory management, caching, disk scheduling, and the I/O subsystem including buffering and spooling.
The storage hierarchy shows the trade-off: faster storage is smaller and more expensive, while slower storage is larger and cheaper.
Here's a detailed comparison: registers are fastest but hold only bytes, while magnetic tape is slowest but stores terabytes at very low cost.
The OS must track which memory is in use, decide what to keep in RAM, and allocate or deallocate memory for processes.
g.text: Which memory is used by whom
What to move in/out of memory

g.data-narration: Caching copies frequently used data to faster storage. When data is needed, we first check the cache - a hit is fast, a miss requires checking slower storage.
In multiprocessor systems, each CPU has its own cache. If CPU1 modifies data in its cache, CPU2's cache becomes stale - this is the coherency problem.
Secondary storage backs up main memory. HDDs use magnetic platters with seek time, while SSDs use flash memory with no moving parts.
g.text: • No moving parts - no seek time
OS manages: Free-space tracking, Storage allocation, I/O scheduling

g.data-narration: The OS schedules disk I/O to minimize seek time. Common algorithms include FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, and C-SCAN - each with different performance trade-offs.
The I/O subsystem hides hardware specifics from users. It includes buffering, caching, spooling, and device drivers for uniform access.
Buffering handles speed mismatches between devices. A buffer stores data temporarily - like a printer buffer holding print jobs while the printer works.
Spooling queues jobs for devices that can't handle interleaved data. Print jobs go to a disk queue, then print one by one - allowing concurrent printing.
Caching speeds up repeated access to data; buffering handles mismatches during transfer. Caching keeps a copy, buffering may hold the only copy.
g.text: Key: Caching = faster access | Buffering = data transfer

g.data-narration: In summary: storage moves from fast/small/expensive registers to slow/large/cheap tape. Memory management tracks and allocates RAM for processes.
For exams: draw the hierarchy pyramid, know buffering vs caching vs spooling differences, and remember the cache coherency solutions.
g.text: Registers → Cache → RAM → SSD → HDD → Tape
Used at every level: hardware, OS, web
